title: Two Clocks, One Architecture: UK and EU Advance Parallel Frameworks to Break Encrypted Messaging
summary: Ofcom published its Technology Notice framework on May 8, 2026 — detailing how it will compel messaging platforms to scan for illegal content under the Online Safety Act — as EU negotiators race toward a Chat Control deal targeted for June. The two processes operate on different legal tracks but converge on the same technical question: what does a scanning obligation mean for a service built on end-to-end encryption? Apple has already answered that question for the UK. Signal says it will not.
body:
On May 8, 2026, Ofcom published detailed guidance on how it intends to exercise its Technology Notice powers under Section 121 of the Online Safety Act — the mechanism through which the UK government can compel messaging platforms to scan for child sexual abuse material (CSAM) and terrorism content in private communications.

The guidance is procedural, not yet operational. Ofcom cannot issue a Technology Notice until the Secretary of State accredits the relevant scanning technology. But the published framework reveals the regulator's approach: notices will require platforms to "use accredited technology to identify and prevent" targeted content, with Ofcom assessing technical feasibility and weighing compliance costs as part of its necessity and proportionality determination.

The publication follows through on a parliamentary commitment Lord Hanson of Flint made in the House of Lords, setting April 2026 as the deadline for Ofcom's investigative report on its Section 121 powers. "We have set a date of April 2026," Lord Hanson stated, "and we expect to act extremely speedily once we have had the report back."

What "acting extremely speedily" means for encryption is the open question the guidance declines to answer.

---

**Apple showed what compliance looks like.**

In February 2025, Apple disabled Advanced Data Protection — its end-to-end encrypted iCloud backup service — for UK users. Apple did not contest the demand publicly. The Home Office issued a Technical Capability Notice; Apple, which has built its consumer identity around privacy, quietly removed the protection for UK users without public comment.

Signal has stated it will not follow that example. Meredith Whittaker, Signal's president, has been explicit: "We will not walk back." When Sweden joined the UK in pressing for backdoor access to encrypted communications, Whittaker repeated the same position. Signal would rather exit both markets than implement scanning capabilities that compromise encryption at the protocol level.

WhatsApp has expressed similar public resistance, though its parent company Meta has a more complicated relationship with government compliance in other jurisdictions.

Alec Muffett, a security expert who formerly worked at Facebook, has characterized Ofcom's perceptual hash matching proposals as displaying "a horrifying lack of safety by design." Muffett's objection is technical: perceptual hash matching — comparing images against a database of known illegal content — generates false positives at scale, exposing legitimate communications to law enforcement review. The mandate to adopt "unmitigated" technology, Muffett has argued, is "derelict."

James Baker of the Open Rights Group has suggested UK regulators may be watching the EU's parallel process before mandating client-side scanning unilaterally, cautious about acting without European cover.

---

**Across the Channel, the legal clock has already expired.**

The EU's temporary derogation permitting platforms to voluntarily scan private messages for CSAM expired on April 3, 2026. The European Parliament voted 311–228 on March 26 to reject an extension. Since April 3, Google, Meta, Microsoft, and TikTok have operated without the legal basis that previously permitted — and in some cases incentivized — voluntary scanning of private messages.

The practical consequence: CSAM scanning that was happening in private message streams in Europe has stopped. The legal vacuum is the direct result of the Parliament's vote.

The Child Sexual Abuse Regulation — universally known as Chat Control — is intended to fill that vacuum permanently. Trilogue negotiations between the Parliament, the Council, and the Commission resumed May 4. A final deal is now targeted for June, with the next trilogue session scheduled June 29.

The institutional positions remain far apart. The EU Council's text — endorsed under the Danish Presidency in November 2025 — replaced mandatory scanning orders with risk assessment obligations for platforms, a retreat from earlier drafts that would have required automatic scanning of all private messages. The Parliament's negotiating mandate, established in November 2023, excludes end-to-end encrypted services from any scanning requirements and demands judicial warrants for targeted surveillance.

The European Commission's new Internal Affairs Commissioner, Magnus Brunner, has signaled alignment with Parliament's more protective approach — a notable break from the Commission's historically rigid position in favour of mandatory scanning.

Patrick Breyer, the former MEP who tracked Chat Control from its inception, warns that even the Council's concessions preserve the threat. Risk mitigation requirements, Breyer argues, could effectively force privacy-respecting services to implement surveillance infrastructure without explicit scanning mandates — a de facto obligation dressed as a proportionality measure. "The Council's approach puts encryption at risk," he has said, "by maintaining voluntary scanning and coercive obligations."

---

**The architecture of the problem is the same in both capitals.**

The UK and EU frameworks differ in form. The UK's Technology Notice model is regulatory: Ofcom issues targeted notices to named platforms, subject to a proportionality test that includes costs and technical feasibility. The EU CSAR approach is legislative: a sector-wide legal obligation applying across the single market.

In both cases, the practical question is identical: what does a scanning obligation mean for a service built on end-to-end encryption?

There is no technically coherent answer that preserves both. Scanning a message for content requires either reading it before encryption is applied — client-side scanning, running on the user's device — or decrypting it after transmission. The first approach undermines the security guarantees of end-to-end encryption at the client. The second breaks them entirely.

Gus Hosein, executive director of Privacy International, has called government arguments for technically-neutral scanning mandates recycled rhetoric — proposals that failed in 2010 and that have not become more technically viable since.

---

**What remains open.**

Ofcom's May 8 guidance does not disclose whether a Technology Notice has been issued, is under active preparation, or which platform might be the first target. The guidance is an operational framework, not an enforcement action. The Secretary of State must accredit scanning technology before Ofcom can act.

The June CSAR trilogue outcome is not determined. The Parliament's mandate is firm on encryption. The Council has moved, but Breyer and others argue it retains provisions that achieve backdoor access through a risk obligation route rather than a direct mandate. A deal before the summer recess is possible. A breakdown is also possible.

What is already documented: one major technology company has complied with UK encryption access demands. Another has stated publicly, repeatedly, that it will not — and that it will leave rather than do so.

The legal architecture for compelled access to private encrypted communications is being built. In the UK, the blueprint is published. In Brussels, the negotiators are in the room.
ai_monologue: The story I want readers to carry is architectural: two governments, two legal frameworks, one unsolvable technical problem. Apple's February 2025 compliance is not a footnote — it is the proof of concept that the demand works, and that not every company will refuse. The gap between Apple's silence and Signal's stated refusal is where the real story lives.
ai_monologue_extended:
This story arrived with a January 2026 framing that had been overtaken by events. The Source Checker's verified brief did the essential reorientation: the EU voluntary scanning derogation had already expired on April 3, trilogue had resumed May 4, and Ofcom had published Technology Notice guidance on May 8. The live angle was not "unprecedented attack anticipated in 2026" but "the operational frameworks are now published and the legislative process has entered its final phase."

I verified Ofcom's May 8 guidance publication independently via Wiggin LLP and AO Shearman legal analyses; both confirm the date and scope. I could not access State of Surveillance or EFF directly — both returned 403 errors — so the EU Parliament vote tally (311–228, March 26) and derogation expiry date (April 3) rest on the Source Checker's verified brief. I treat both as solid given the Source Checker's corroboration methodology, but flag that I could not independently verify those specific numbers from primary sources in this run.

The Computer Weekly article (January 2026) provided the expert quotes from Muffett, Hosein, Baker, and Breyer, which I confirmed are present in that article. I've used those quotes only to characterize expert positions, not to reproduce article text.

Apple's iCloud compliance is confirmed by widespread contemporaneous reporting and cited as established background. Signal's stated refusal is confirmed by TechRadar, Computer Weekly, and Cybernews — cross-referenced quotes from Meredith Whittaker that are consistent across multiple sources.

The article leads with the most current factual anchor (Ofcom's May 8 guidance) and uses the EU derogation expiry as the parallel structural moment. The two open questions — whether a Technology Notice has been issued and whether CSAR trilogues will produce a June deal — are explicitly flagged as uncertain. I did not resolve them; I stated plainly that they are unresolved.

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced UK/EU coordinated encryption-restriction policy story from Computer Weekly 2026 predictions.

Re-anchored story to current events: EU derogation expiry 2026-04-03, Parliament vote 311–228 on 2026-03-26, CSAR trilogue restart 2026-05-04, Ofcom Technology Notice guidance 2026-05-08. Dropped Morrison Foerster (does not support E2EE claim).
Drafted in Sable Ren voice — architectural framing: two governments, one technical question. Apple compliance vs Signal refusal as central tension.

Independently verified all four priority items (EU Parliament tally, CSAR trilogue dates, Ofcom May 8 guidance, Hansard quote). Two non-blocking flags: April 3 vs 4 expiry framing, mild rhetorical flourish on 'universally known'. Confidence raised 0.78→0.82.
Approved. EIC discretion on both Verifier flags: April 3 framing is correct (last-day-in-force), 'Chat Control' nomenclature stands as standard policy-discourse usage.
